title: Lonchocarpus maculatus
taxonomy: Lonchocarpus maculatus belongs to the family Fabaceae, also known as the legume family. This classification is based on the plant's characteristics, including its fruit and leaf morphology. The genus Lonchocarpus is a group of flowering plants that are native to Central and South America. These plants are known for their small, yellow flowers and long, flat seed pods. As a member of the Fabaceae family, Lonchocarpus maculatus is closely related to other plants like beans and peas.
uses: Lonchocarpus maculatus is a plant with reported medicinal uses, particularly in traditional medicine. The plant's extracts have been studied for their potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. In some regions, it is used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. However, further research is needed to fully understand its medicinal properties and potential applications. More studies are necessary to confirm its effectiveness and safety for human use.

parts: Lonchocarpus maculatus, also known as the spotted Lonchocarpus, is a plant native to Central and South America. The medicinal parts of this plant are primarily its seeds, which contain a toxic compound called rotenone. Rotenone has been used for its insecticidal properties and as a natural pest control agent. However, the seeds are also used in traditional medicine to treat various health issues, such as fever and skin infections. When used properly and in moderation, the seeds are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.

side_effects: Lonchocarpus maculatus, a plant species, is reported to have potential side effects due to the presence of toxic compounds such as rotenone and other alkaloids. These compounds can cause gastrointestinal problems, nausea, and vomiting when ingested. Additionally, prolonged exposure to rotenone can lead to neurological damage and other systemic issues.
